did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a deep learning-based Convolutional Neural Network (DLCNN) architecture named SCC-Net for skin cancer detection and multi-class classification. The model was trained on the ISIC-2019 dataset to classify nine types of skin diseases, achieving 93.08% accuracy.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled 'ORIGINAL RESEARCH' in the header. It presents a novel deep learning architecture (SCC-Net), describes its methodology (data preprocessing, model training on ISIC-2019 dataset), and reports original experimental results including accuracy (93.08%), precision, recall, and F1-score, along with an ablation study and comparison with state-of-the-art models.

title: SCCNet: Skin Cancer Detection and Multi-Class Classification Using Deep CNN Model with Estimated Disease Probabilities
brief_summary: This study proposes SCC-Net, a deep learning-based deep convolutional neural network (DLCNN) architecture for skin cancer detection and multi-class classification of nine distinct skin diseases. The model was trained on the publicly available ISIC-2019 dataset, utilizing image preprocessing, small kernel sizes for computational efficiency, and Adam optimization to minimize binary cross-entropy losses. The primary evaluation approach involved comparing the proposed model's performance against state-of-the-art deep learning models and traditional machine learning methods using metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score. The results demonstrated that SCC-Net achieved a classification accuracy of 93.08%, precision of 89.50%, recall of 92.28%, and an F1-score of 90.36%. These findings indicate that the proposed method outperforms existing approaches, offering a robust framework for early skin lesion diagnosis.

all_task_types.target: Nine distinct types of skin diseases (Basal cell carcinoma, Actinic keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Melanoma, Nevus, Pigmented benign keratosis, Squamous cell carcinoma, Seborrheic keratosis, Vascular lesion)
all_task_types.details: Multi-class classification of skin lesions using the ISIC-2019 dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (disease labels) using a SoftMax activation function. The output includes estimated disease probabilities (EDP) for each of the nine classes, where the class with the maximum probability is selected as the prediction. Performance was evaluated using accuracy (93.08%), precision (89.50%), recall (92.28%), and F1-score (90.36%).

datasets_used.name: ISIC-2019
datasets_used.description: The International Skin Imaging Collaboration 2019 dataset, a public collection of dermoscopy skin lesion images with diagnostic categories, patient age, sex, and anatomic location metadata. Images are stored in JPEG format.

use.training: 80% of the dataset (20,480 images) was used to train the DLCNN model in a web-based cloud environment.
use.testing: 20% of the dataset (5,120 images) was used to evaluate the model's performance against state-of-the-art models.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using absolute metric values (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) and percentage improvements over baseline models without statistical significance testing.

ISIC-2019.notes: The proposed SCC-Net outperformed state-of-the-art DL models and traditional ML methods. Specific baseline metric values for MCML, ANN, and DNN were not explicitly reported in the text, only the percentage improvements achieved by SCC-Net over them. The ablation study compared SCC-Net variants (fewer layers, no optimization) against the proposed model. Table 3 in the paper lists baseline metrics as: MCML (Accuracy 86.47%, Precision 87.82%, Recall 91.01%, F1 88.48), ANN (Accuracy 87.82%, Precision 88.183%, Recall 91.37%, F1 89.19), DNN (Accuracy 88.183%, Precision 89.50%, Recall 91.75%, F1 89.92). Note: The text description of improvements seems to have slight inconsistencies with the table values provided in Chunk 23, but the table values are the direct reported metrics for baselines.

architecture_used.main_model: SCC-Net (Deep Learning-based Convolutional Neural Network / DLCNN)
architecture_used.input_size: 64x64 pixels (standardized size for preprocessed skin images)

architecture_details.notes: The paper mentions the DLCNN method includes six convolution layers in one section, but Table 1 details a specific architecture with two convolutional layers (Conv1, Conv2) followed by pooling and dense layers. The model uses small kernel sizes to reduce parameters.

training_details.regularization: Dropout regularization (mentioned in abstract to prevent overfitting)
training_details.preprocessing: Normalization of pixel values to [0,1] or [-1,1], resizing to 64x64 pixels

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: SCC-Net (Proposed Deep Learning-based Convolutional Neural Network / DLCNN)
preprocessing_techniques: Normalization of pixel values to a predetermined range such as [0,1] or [-1,1]
Resizing images to a standardized size of 64x64 pixels
Patch extraction (dividing images into patches of size 3x3 with a stride of 1 pixel)
Removal of background noise (mentioned as an adaptation of pre-processing methods)
Data augmentation techniques including random rotations, flips, and color transformations (mentioned as typical preparation procedures for the ISIC-2019 dataset)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Mentioned as a typical preparation procedure/data augmentation technique for the ISIC-2019 dataset to enlarge training data and enhance variety.

conclusions: The proposed SCC-Net deep learning-based convolutional neural network (DLCNN) architecture achieved a classification accuracy of 93.08%, precision of 89.50%, recall of 92.28%, and an F1-score of 90.36% on the ISIC-2019 dataset for multi-class skin cancer detection.
SCC-Net outperformed several state-of-the-art deep learning models and traditional machine learning methods, including MCML, ANN, and DNN, in terms of accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.
The use of small kernel sizes in the convolutional layers of SCC-Net effectively reduced the number of parameters and increased computational efficiency.
Adam optimization was effective in minimizing binary cross-entropy losses during the training phase.
Dropout regularization helped prevent overfitting and improved the overall robustness of the DLCNN model.
The study demonstrated the feasibility of implementing a web-based environment for uploading skin lesion images and predicting nine different types of skin cancers.

The proposed framework offers a robust solution for early skin lesion diagnosis, addressing the gap between the high prevalence of skin diseases and the limited availability of specialized medical resources.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the ISIC-2019 dataset for both training and testing, with no external validation on independent clinical datasets, limiting the assessment of generalizability to real-world clinical scenarios.
The paper acknowledges that dataset biases or inaccuracies in the training data could adversely affect model performance and lead to erroneous classifications in clinical practice.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed; performance comparisons against baselines were based solely on absolute metric values.
The model architecture description contains inconsistencies: the text mentions six convolutional layers, while Table 1 details an architecture with only two convolutional layers.
The paper notes that Adam optimization does not always converge to the global optimum, which may result in suboptimal model performance.
The study highlights the 'black-box' nature of deep learning models as a significant barrier to clinical adoption due to lack of interpretability and explainability.

The dataset exhibits significant class imbalance, with 'Nevus' comprising 12,875 samples compared to 'Dermatofibroma' (239), 'Seborrheic keratosis' (269), and 'Vascular lesion' (253), which may bias the model towards majority classes.
Computational complexity and resource requirements for training deep learning models are cited as potential limitations for deployment in resource-constrained settings.
The proposed web-based environment is a proof-of-concept implementation; its clinical utility and robustness in routine diagnostic practices remain unverified.
